title: Antinuclear antibodies (ANA)
body_html:
Background
Antinuclear antibodies (ANA) are a family of autoantibodies directed against components of the cell nucleus, including DNA, RNA, and associated proteins. They are a hallmark of autoimmune diseases. They are sometimes referred to as antinuclear factors (ANF).

Clinical Significance:
ANA are nonspecific but are strongly associated with autoimmune connective tissue diseases. ANA subtypes matter for specificity (see table below).

A positive ANA raises suspicion for connective tissue disease, but is not diagnostic. Subtype testing (anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm, anti-Ro/La, anti-Scl-70, etc.) narrows diagnosis.

ConditionFrequency of ANATypical Notes
Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE)>95%Often homogeneous or speckled pattern; anti-dsDNA highly specific
Sjögren’s syndrome60–80%Anti-SSA/Ro and anti-SSB/La common
Systemic sclerosis (Scleroderma)60–90%Often nucleolar or centromere pattern
Mixed connective tissue disease (MCTD)100%High titre anti-U1-RNP
Rheumatoid arthritis30–50%Usually low titre; not diagnostic
Polymyositis / Dermatomyositis20–50%Anti-Jo-1 plus various other ENAs

Interpretation
ANA can be positive in healthy individuals, especially elderly (up to 20% may have low titres). Titre and pattern are important.

Titre:
  • Low titre (1:40 or 1:80) may be nonspecific
  • High titre (≥1:160) is more clinically significant

Pattern recognition: The staining pattern can give clues about the specific autoantibody present:
  • Homogeneous: often anti-dsDNA or histone
  • Speckled: often anti-Sm, anti-RNP, anti-SSA/SSB
  • Nucleolar: systemic sclerosis
  • Centromere: limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis (CREST)

Clinical uses:
  • Screening for autoimmune rheumatic diseases
  • Supporting diagnosis in combination with clinical features
  • Monitoring disease activity (specific antibodies like anti-dsDNA in SLE)

Key Features:
Type: Autoantibodies (IgG most common)
Target: Nuclear antigens such as:
  • DNA (double-stranded, dsDNA)
  • Histones
  • Extractable nuclear antigens (ENAs) like SSA/Ro, SSB/La, RNP, Sm
  • Nucleolar proteins
Detection: Typically by indirect immunofluorescence (IIF) on HEp-2 cells, ELISA, or multiplex assays.

Main ANA subtypes
ANA subtype classAntibodies
1. DNA-associatedAnti-dsDNA → highly specific for SLE, correlates with nephritis
Anti-ssDNA → less specific, sometimes in SLE or other CTDs
2. Histone-associatedAnti-histone → classic for drug-induced lupus
3. RNA-binding proteins (Extractable Nuclear Antigens, ENAs)Anti-Sm → highly specific for SLE (though less common)
Anti-RNP (U1-RNP) → mixed connective tissue disease (MCTD), also SLE
Anti-SSA (Ro) → Sjögren’s syndrome, SLE (especially subacute cutaneous lupus)
Anti-SSB (La) → Sjögren’s syndrome
Anti-Scl-70 (topoisomerase I) → diffuse systemic sclerosis
Anti-centromere → limited systemic sclerosis (CREST)
Anti-PM/Scl → overlap of myositis and scleroderma
Anti-Jo-1 (histidyl tRNA synthetase) → polymyositis/dermatomyositis
Other myositis-associated ENAs → anti-Mi-2, anti-SRP, anti-TIF1γ, etc.
4. Nucleolar antibodies (often systemic sclerosis)Anti-fibrillarin (U3-RNP)
Anti-Th/To
Anti-RNA polymerase I/III
5. Other ANA targetsAnti-PCNA (proliferating cell nuclear antigen) → rare, some SLE cases
Anti-Ku → overlap syndromes
5
400
Pathology
Rheumatology, dermatology & women's health
